reasoning: The verb changes with the type of food: loʔ for soft/ripe/sweet items (honey, ripe mango, bananas), k’ux for hard/uncooked/grainy items (beans, corn, raw greens, unripe mango), tiʔ for animal parts and chiles, weʔ for bread/tortilla/tamales, and chik’ for soups/soaked foods. Avocado patterns with loʔ despite not being sweet, explicitly noted in the prompt, so it is the exception.
j- marks 1sg; tiʔ is used with body parts; sk’in wakax means ‘cow’s kidney’. Thus: la jtiʔ sk’in wakax = ‘I ate cow kidney.’
‘We’ uses j- … -tik. Peanuts behave like beans (hard/crunchy) → k’ux class. Definite ‘the’ takes te and final -e: ‘te kaxlan chenek’e’.
‘You (pl.)’ parallels other plural subjects with -ik. Stomach is a body part → tiʔ class. Animal body parts take a 3rd-person possessor s- before the noun: stsukum wakax ‘cow stomach’.
‘They’ is s- … -ik. Meat is animal flesh, patterned with body parts → tiʔ class.
‘You (sg.)’ takes a-. Avocados pattern with loʔ. Possessed ‘your (pl.)’ uses a- on the noun with plural possessor -nik; with avocado this yields aʔonik.
‘I’ uses j-. Honey is soft/sweet → loʔ class. Possessed ‘my’ on the noun uses j-: jchab.
Cooked greens are soft/mushy, so use the loʔ class with 3sg subject s-: sloʔ bok.
Soups/soaked foods use chik’. With 3sg subject s- + chik’ → schik’. ‘Bean soup’ is chenek’ tiʔbal.
Marrow is soft, so it uses the loʔ verb; for 1sg the form is jloʔ, not jtiʔ.
Toasted tortillas are crispy/hard, so they take the k’ux-class verb; with 3sg subject this is sk’ux, not sweʔ.
kaxlan means ‘foreign/Spanish’ (cf. kaxlan waj = bread, kaxlan chenek’ = peanuts ‘foreign beans’). Therefore kaxlan ixim = ‘foreign corn’ = wheat.
